articles.title: Building from Zero After Addiction, Prison, and a Felony
articles.summary: Building from Zero After Addiction, Prison, and a Felony I spent ages 14-16 in a maximum-security juvenile prison, became a felon at 19, lost almost everything to addiction, and later rebuilt my life through software, open source, and a few people who took a chance on me. I've wanted to write this for a while but kept finding reasons not to. It felt too personal, too risky, and too easy to misread. Recently, I decided on two things: After seeing Preston Thorpe speak publicly about his own background, I wondered how many others like us were silently lurking in tech. My hope is that it serves as encouragement to others who are in similar circumstances that things CAN get better.

articles.title: My automated doubt development process
articles.summary: Automated doubt development processes can help regain trust in AI-assisted development by ensuring that all aspects of a system are thoroughly critiqued and scrutinized from multiple perspectives. This process involves automating the identification of potential issues through various agents, such as pre-implementation architects, documentation validators, assumption excavators, gap analyzers, and implied completeness detectors. By incorporating these tools into the development workflow, developers can catch defects and inconsistencies that might otherwise go unnoticed, leading to a more robust and reliable system.

articles.title: Entropy
articles.summary: Entropy is a system within Linux that feeds randomness from user-space data sources into its entropy pool. This can be achieved by writing data to the /dev/{u}random device on a Linux system. The entropy pool is replenished through various mechanisms, including seeding with data from other systems and re-seeding at boot time. While it may not be necessary for most users, this feature provides an interesting opportunity for experimentation with plugin-based systems like WASM.

articles.title: Powering up a module from the IBM 604: an electronic calculator from 1948
articles.summary: The IBM 604 Electronic Calculating Punch used electronic technology to power its module, which combined a vacuum-tube circuitry with a socketed plugboard. This innovation led to the development of more powerful computers in the 1950s, including those that used vacuum tubes like the Manchester Baby and ENIAC. The pluggable module was an important finding, as it allowed for easy replacement of faulty components without having to dismantle the entire calculator.

articles.title: Getting silly with C, part &((int*)-8)[3]
articles.summary: Researchers have discovered a potential vulnerability in the C programming language that could allow attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected systems. The issue is related to the use of a pointer arithmetic operation involving an integer and a negative value. This type of attack can be particularly damaging because it doesn't require the attacker to know the exact location of sensitive data, making it harder to defend against. Developers who write C code should take note of this finding to ensure their applications are secure.

articles.title: Ergonomic overrides for Nixpkgs
articles.summary: Ergonomic overrides for Nixpkgs are a new package that simplifies the process of overriding dependencies in Nixpkgs, which is part of a broader effort to improve usability. This project aims to address the fundamental problem of type-checking Nix, where nobody actually uses it at any significant scale, by creating embedded languages within Nix for programming "in the large". By designing better abstractions, such as override functions and overlays, developers can build more user-friendly tools that work well in inexpert hands.

articles.title: The perils of UUID primary keys in SQLite
articles.summary: Random UUIDs can cause significant extra paging in clustered indexes, leading to poor performance. This is because the random nature of UUIDs means that rows are inserted into the B-tree in a seemingly random order, requiring re-balancing which can be time-consuming. The use of random UUIDs as primary keys can lead to wasted space and increased latency in databases like SQLite.

articles.title: What is the purpose of the lost+found folder in Linux and Unix? (2014)
articles.summary: The lost+found folder in Linux and Unix operating systems is a directory used to store incomplete or deleted files. It is typically found at the root of the file system and contains data fragments that are not referenced anywhere in the filesystem. These fragments can be repaired by running the fsck command, which will turn them back into complete files. The lost+found folder is specific to Linux extended file systems such as ext2-4, but not typically used on Unix systems like FreeBSD or OpenBSD.
